title: The White Disease
description: A political allegory linking epidemic fear to dictatorship, militarism, and moral cowardice.
descriptionParagraphs: A political allegory linking epidemic fear to dictatorship, militarism, and moral cowardice.

The White Disease stages anti fascism, allegory, and war critique through conflict, speech, and performance. As a dramatic work, much of its force comes from what characters say in public, conceal in private, and embody on the stage.

What keeps the work alive is the way argument becomes performance. Its themes stay vivid because they are enacted through timing, irony, confrontation, and the tension between private desire and public order.

overview.englishDescription: A political allegory linking epidemic fear to dictatorship, militarism, and moral cowardice.
banningParagraphs: The White Disease entered censorship debates as a play associated with anti fascism, allegory, and war critique. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political dissent and anti militarism.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1938 in Czechoslovakia, where Second Czechoslovak Republic authorities banned circulation. The state blocked a play whose anti-authoritarian message collided with the crisis of the late 1930s. Its censorship came at the exact historical moment the play warned against.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
counterReadings.author: Hannah Arendt
counterReadings.note: A foundational analysis of state terror, propaganda, and ideological conformity.

counterReadings.title: On Tyranny
counterReadings.author: Timothy Snyder
counterReadings.note: A short modern guide to resisting authoritarian politics and controlled public discourse.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
